case_metadata.user_request: Find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for the adsorbate with SMILES O=N on the provided surface.

This is a new configuration not yet tested.

plan.reasoning:
SMILES Consistency Check: The SMILES is O=N, which represents a nitroso-like fragment (NO). RDKit analysis shows: atom 0 is O (SP2, degree 1, no radical electrons), atom 1 is N (SP2, degree 1, no radical electrons). Both atoms have no radical electrons, so this is treated as a Molecule (stable NO species). The molecule has a double bond between O and N, with both ends being terminal atoms capable of donating lone pairs to the surface.

Adsorbate Analysis: NO (O=N) is a well-known surface adsorbate. It can bind through N (index 1) or O (index 0) in an end-on configuration, or side-on through both atoms. N-end binding is typically preferred on transition metal surfaces (Ti is present here). The surface contains Ti, In, and N atoms.

Surface Analysis: Available sites include ontop (In, N, Ti), bridge (In-In, In-Ti, N-N, N-Ti, Ti-Ti), hollow-3 (In-In-Ti, In-N-Ti, N-Ti-Ti, Ti-Ti-Ti), hollow-4 (In-N-Ti-Ti), and 5-fold (N-N-Ti-Ti-Ti).

Strategy: Since this is the first attempt, I will start with the most chemically intuitive configuration: N-end (index 1) ontop adsorption on Ti, as Ti is the most reactive transition metal on this surface and N-end binding of NO on Ti is well-established in catalysis literature. This is the most likely lowest-energy configuration to start with.

Plan: site_type=ontop, surface_binding_atoms=['Ti'], adsorbate_binding_indices=[1] (N atom binds to Ti).

plan.reasoning: SMILES Consistency Check: O=N is nitroso (or NO radical). RDKit shows O at index 0 (SP2, no radical electrons) and N at index 1 (SP2, no radical electrons). Both atoms have degree 1 and no radical electrons, so this is treated as a Molecule. The molecule has a double bond between O and N, with N being the typical binding atom for NO on metal surfaces (N-down configuration is common). History Analysis: The only attempt was ontop @ Ti (index 1, N-down), which dissociated and ended up in a hollow (Ti,Ti,Ti) site with E=-11.352 eV. This is a failed attempt due to dissociation. New Plan: I should try a different site and/or orientation. Since N-down on Ti ontop led to dissociation, I will try: 1) O-down (O at index 0) on a Ti ontop site - this is a less reactive orientation that may prevent dissociation. Alternatively, 2) Try a bridge site with N binding. Let me try N-down on an In ontop site, as In is less reactive than Ti and may stabilize the molecule without dissociation. In is available as an ontop site. Using N (index 1) as the binding atom on In ontop. This is a completely new plan different from the previous Ti ontop attempt.

plan.reasoning: Analysis of history: 1) ontop@Ti -> dissociated (failed); 2) ontop@In -> bridge(Ti,Ti), E=-7.881 eV (success); 3) bridge@In-Ti -> hollow(FCC, In,Ti,Ti), E=-6.971 eV (success); 4) hollow@N-Ti-Ti -> hollow(HCP, In,Ti,Ti,Ti,Ti), E=-8.409 eV (current best, success). The adsorbate O=N has O at index 0 (SP2, no radical) and N at index 1 (SP2, no radical). Both atoms are chemically valid donor atoms. The best result so far is E=-8.409 eV from hollow@N-Ti-Ti with N as binding atom (index 1). I have not yet tried: (a) hollow sites with O as the binding atom, (b) ontop@N site, (c) bridge@N-N or N-Ti with O binding, or (d) 5-fold N-N-Ti-Ti-Ti site. The 5-fold site (N-N-Ti-Ti-Ti) is a unique high-coordination site not yet explored. Given that the hollow site gave the best energy, a higher-coordination 5-fold site could potentially give even lower energy. I will try the 5-fold site N-N-Ti-Ti-Ti with N as the binding atom (index 1), using hollow as the site_type (since 5-fold maps to hollow in canonical naming). This is a completely new plan not tried before. The surface has N atoms available in the 5-fold site combination. Using N binding (index 1) is consistent with the best result so far.
